refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-005: The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements from worksite mission context.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates three distinct obligations: (1) deriving requirements from context, (2) recording them, and (3) managing them over time. Derivation is the primary functional obligation in the problem domain; recording and managing are implementation concerns that belong in the DP/IM layers. The refined FR focuses on the core functional need: translating worksite mission inputs into explicit performance targets. This remains solution-neutral and avoids prescribing 'method,' 'document,' or 'history' which are design choices.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that given a worksite mission context (e.g., site layout, production targets, safety constraints), the system produces a traceable set of performance requirements (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) that satisfy those inputs. Acceptance criteria: requirements are documented, baselined, and linked to source mission data.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: The NGHE solution shall accept worksite mission context inputs (e.g., production targets, site constraints, safety policies).
CR-NGHE-005.2: The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements (e.g., cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) that satisfy the worksite mission context.
CR-NGHE-005.3: The NGHE solution shall record and maintain traceability between derived performance requirements and their source mission context.
CR-NGHE-005.4: The NGHE solution shall maintain a history of performance requirement baselines and calibration updates.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a mission-context capture module that accepts worksite inputs (safety plan, operational policies, environmental conditions) and applies a rule-based inference engine to derive performance targets; maintain a versioned baseline repository with change tracking.

DP-B: Establish a hierarchical performance requirement decomposition process where worksite mission context is mapped to system-level performance targets through a documented traceability matrix; store all versions in a configuration management system.

DP-C: Design a parameterized performance model that accepts worksite mission context as input variables and computes performance targets via a calibrated mathematical model; log all model inputs, outputs, and calibration events.

DP-D: Create a manual performance requirement specification workflow where domain experts review worksite context and document performance targets in a structured template; implement a change control board to approve and version all updates.

DP-E: Implement a hybrid approach combining automated context parsing (DP-A logic) with expert validation gates (DP-D oversight) to derive performance targets; maintain dual-layer traceability (automated derivation + expert approval) and full audit history.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-E: Implement a hybrid approach combining automated context parsing with expert validation gates to derive performance targets; maintain dual-layer traceability (automated derivation + expert approval) and full audit history.

potential_sources: Coupling between context capture module and traceability matrix if schema changes are not versioned independently.
Coupling between performance model and baseline repository if model updates are not decoupled from historical baseline storage.
Coupling between expert validation workflow and automated inference if approval logic is hardcoded rather than configurable.
coupling_check.notes: DP-E mitigates coupling by separating concerns: (1) automated derivation logic is isolated from (2) expert validation gates, and (3) traceability/audit is orthogonal to both. The dual-layer approach allows independent evolution of inference rules and approval policies. Risk: if validation gate criteria are tightly bound to specific performance model assumptions, decoupling may be compromised. Mitigation: define validation gate criteria as independent configuration, not embedded in model code.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Worksite mission context inputs are available in a structured or semi-structured format (e.g., safety plan document, operational policy database). RISK: If inputs are unstructured narrative text, automated parsing will require NLP or manual pre-processing, increasing complexity and error rate.

ASSUMPTION: Expert validators are available and responsive to approve derived performance targets within acceptable lead time. RISK: If validation becomes a bottleneck, the system may not meet operational agility requirements.

ASSUMPTION: Performance targets derived from mission context are deterministic or have well-defined uncertainty bounds. RISK: If mission context is ambiguous or contradictory, the system may produce conflicting or non-traceable targets.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies are maintained in a controlled configuration. RISK: If policies change frequently without formal change control, traceability to performance targets may become stale or incorrect.
ASSUMPTION: Baseline and calibration history is required for compliance or audit purposes. RISK: If retention policy is not defined, storage and retrieval of historical data may become unmanageable.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR-NGHE-005 (refined): The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements from worksite mission context via a hybrid process that (1) automatically parses worksite inputs (safety plan, operational policies, environmental conditions) to generate candidate performance targets, (2) subjects candidates to expert validation and approval, (3) produces a documented, versioned set of performance targets with full traceability to source inputs and approval records, and (4) maintains a queryable history of all baselines, calibration updates, and policy changes. Acceptance criteria: (a) Every performance target is traceable to at least one worksite input and one expert approval record. (b) All baseline and calibration updates are logged with timestamp, actor, and rationale. (c) System supports rollback to any prior baseline version. (d) Traceability reports are generated on demand and include derivation chain and validation evidence.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized module responsible for performance target derivation, validation gating, traceability management, and audit logging for safety-critical system parameters

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives raw operational context data (vehicle state, environmental conditions, mission parameters) to feed automated context parsing

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outputs validated performance targets and derived constraints to guide vehicle and teleoperation subsystems

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional: receives expert validation requests and approval/rejection signals; emits audit trail and traceability records for compliance

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: Safety Services is the natural owner of validation gates, audit history, and dual-layer traceability. Expert validation and performance target derivation are core safety functions. Minor diffusion: Telemetry ingestion and Tasking output are external dependencies, but both are well-defined interfaces.

Moderate fit: NGHE is a high-level orchestration layer. While it could coordinate the derivation process, it would diffuse ownership of validation gates and audit logic across multiple subsystems. Traceability and expert approval would leak into Vehicle and Teleoperation.

Poor fit: Vehicle is a physical/operational execution module, not a governance or validation layer. Placing context parsing, expert validation, and audit history here violates separation of concerns. High diffusion: safety logic would be entangled with vehicle control.

Poor fit: Teleoperation is a human-in-the-loop interface module. While it may consume validated targets, it should not own the derivation, validation gating, or audit trail. High diffusion: safety-critical logic would scatter across operator interface and backend.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the single authoritative owner of performance target derivation and validation because: (1) it is the designated safety-critical governance layer; (2) expert validation gates are inherently safety functions; (3) dual-layer traceability and audit history are compliance and safety assurance artifacts; (4) all three concerns (automated parsing, expert approval, audit logging) are cohesive within a safety validation domain; (5) external interfaces (Telemetry in, Tasking out, SafetyAlert bidirectional) are clean and well-scoped.
containment_risks: Risk: If expert validation is performed by human operators in Teleoperation, approval signals must flow through SafetyAlert interface to Safety Services to maintain centralized audit trail. Ensure no approval logic is duplicated in Teleoperation.
Risk: Automated context parsing may require real-time performance; Safety Services must not become a bottleneck. Consider asynchronous derivation with caching if latency is critical.
Risk: Audit history growth could become unbounded. Establish retention policies and archival strategy within Safety Services to prevent storage overflow.
Risk: If Vehicle or Teleoperation subsystems bypass Safety Services to derive their own targets, traceability is lost. Enforce interface contracts strictly.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-E correctly addresses FR-NGHE-005 by specifying a hybrid approach (automated parsing + expert validation) that directly derives performance requirements from worksite mission context. The dual-layer traceability and audit history satisfy the documented traceability and history maintenance acceptance criteria. The DP is solution-agnostic enough to permit multiple implementation strategies while remaining functionally complete.

diffusion_risks: Expert validation approval signals must flow exclusively through SafetyAlert interface to Safety Services; if Teleoperation operators approve targets locally without routing through Safety Services, audit trail fragmentation and dual-approval logic will diffuse the DP across modules.

Automated context parsing latency could create a bottleneck in Safety Services; if Vehicle or Teleoperation subsystems implement local caching or fallback parsing to avoid latency, the DP diffuses and traceability is lost. Establish SLA and consider asynchronous derivation with pre-computed baseline caching.

Audit history retention and archival logic must remain entirely within Safety Services; if audit records are replicated or archived by external modules (e.g., Telemetry, Tasking), consistency and single-source-of-truth are compromised.

Telemetry ingestion is a clean inbound interface, but if Telemetry performs any filtering, normalization, or context enrichment before delivery to Safety Services, the automated parsing responsibility diffuses. Ensure Telemetry delivers raw operational context only.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Safety Services is the optimal IM boundary with a containment score of 0.92. The module cleanly owns performance target derivation, expert validation gating, dual-layer traceability, and audit logging—all cohesive safety-critical governance functions. External interfaces are well-scoped: Telemetry (in) supplies raw context, Tasking (out) receives validated targets, SafetyAlert (bidirectional) handles expert approval and compliance records. Minor diffusion (0.08) arises only from external dependencies, which are unavoidable and properly abstracted. Alternative candidates (NGHE Solution, Vehicle, Teleoperation) exhibit poor containment (0.35–0.65) and high diffusion (0.35–0.65) due to violation of separation of concerns and scattering of safety logic.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Safety Services can be instrumented to log all derivation steps (input context, parsing decisions, expert approvals, final targets) with timestamps and actor identities. Acceptance criteria 'documented set of performance targets traceable to worksite inputs' is directly verifiable via audit trail inspection. Acceptance criteria 'maintains a history of baselines/calibration updates' is verifiable by querying Safety Services audit history for all target revisions and their justifications.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. Safety Services is the single authoritative owner of FR-NGHE-005 realization. Responsibilities are unambiguous: (1) ingest raw context from Telemetry, (2) execute automated parsing logic, (3) gate expert validation via SafetyAlert, (4) maintain dual-layer traceability (automated derivation + approval), (5) log audit trail. Interfaces are explicit: Telemetry supplies inputs, Tasking consumes outputs, SafetyAlert mediates expert approval. No ambiguity regarding which module owns which aspect of the DP.

recommended_revisions: Enforce interface contract: Telemetry must deliver raw operational context (vehicle state, environmental conditions, mission parameters) without pre-processing; any filtering or enrichment must occur within Safety Services to preserve traceability of derivation logic.

Define latency SLA for automated context parsing within Safety Services; if real-time performance is critical, implement asynchronous derivation with pre-computed baseline caching to prevent bottleneck without diffusing the DP.

Establish audit history retention and archival policy within Safety Services (e.g., rolling window, compression, cold storage) to prevent unbounded growth and ensure compliance with worksite safety plan record-keeping requirements.

Mandate that all expert validation approvals flow through SafetyAlert interface to Safety Services; prohibit local approval logic in Teleoperation or other subsystems to maintain centralized audit trail and prevent dual-approval diffusion.

Add explicit constraint to DP-E: 'No subsystem (Vehicle, Teleoperation, NGHE Solution) shall derive or cache performance targets independently; all targets must originate from Safety Services to ensure single source of truth and traceability.'

Document the mapping from worksite safety plan and operational policies (input constraints) to automated parsing rules and expert validation criteria within Safety Services design specification to close the loop between FR acceptance criteria and IM realization.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence and clear allocation to Safety Services. However, the DP-IM gate identifies five concrete diffusion risks that require decomposition into sub-FRs to enforce architectural boundaries and prevent capability leakage across module interfaces. The recommended revisions are architectural constraints that must be realized as lower-level functional requirements governing interface contracts, latency behavior, audit governance, and approval routing. Safety criticality (high) mandates explicit decomposition of these constraints into verifiable sub-requirements rather than relying on design-time documentation alone. Proceeding to next-level decomposition will operationalize the diffusion containment strategy and establish measurable acceptance criteria for each interface and internal governance function.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-005-A: Enforce raw context ingestion contract—Telemetry shall deliver unprocessed operational context (vehicle state, environmental conditions, mission parameters) to Safety Services without filtering, normalization, or enrichment; any pre-processing by Telemetry shall be prohibited to preserve traceability of automated parsing logic within Safety Services.

FR-NGHE-005-B: Establish automated parsing latency SLA—Safety Services shall derive performance targets from raw context within a defined latency threshold (e.g., <500ms for real-time operations); if latency exceeds threshold, Safety Services shall implement asynchronous derivation with pre-computed baseline caching to prevent bottleneck without diffusing DP across Vehicle or Teleoperation subsystems.

FR-NGHE-005-C: Centralize expert validation approval routing—All expert validation approvals for performance targets shall flow exclusively through SafetyAlert interface to Safety Services; Teleoperation operators and other subsystems shall be prohibited from approving targets locally to maintain centralized audit trail and prevent dual-approval logic diffusion.

FR-NGHE-005-D: Maintain single source of truth for performance targets—No subsystem (Vehicle, Teleoperation, NGHE Solution) shall derive, cache, or modify performance targets independently; all targets shall originate exclusively from Safety Services and be distributed via Tasking interface to ensure single source of truth and end-to-end traceability.

FR-NGHE-005-E: Govern audit history retention and archival—Safety Services shall own all audit history retention, archival, and compliance logic for performance target derivation and approval records; external modules (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert) shall not replicate, filter, or archive audit records to preserve consistency and single-source-of-truth integrity.

FR-NGHE-005-F: Map safety plan policies to parsing rules—Safety Services design specification shall document the explicit mapping from worksite safety plan and operational policies (input constraints) to automated parsing rules and expert validation criteria to close the loop between FR acceptance criteria and IM realization and enable traceability audits.

revise.why: No revision of current-level FR, DP, or IM is required. All three gates pass with clear rationale and high containment score (0.92). The recommended revisions are architectural constraints that should be operationalized as sub-level FRs rather than modifications to the current level. Revising at the current level would obscure the hierarchical decomposition and delay realization of diffusion containment.

assumptions: Telemetry subsystem is capable of delivering raw, unprocessed operational context without pre-filtering or enrichment.
Safety Services has sufficient computational capacity to execute automated parsing and expert validation gating within defined latency SLA.
SafetyAlert interface is bidirectional and capable of routing expert approval signals back to Safety Services with full audit trail.
Tasking subsystem will consume performance targets exclusively from Safety Services and will not implement local caching or fallback derivation logic.
Worksite safety plan and operational policies are documented and available as input constraints to Safety Services design specification.
Expert validation approval process is synchronous or can be made asynchronous with acceptable latency trade-offs.
Audit history retention and archival policies are compatible with worksite safety plan record-keeping requirements.
open_risks: Latency bottleneck in Safety Services automated parsing could trigger unauthorized local caching or fallback logic in Vehicle or Teleoperation subsystems if SLA is not enforced with monitoring and alerting.

Expert validation approval signals may be duplicated or routed through multiple interfaces (SafetyAlert, Teleoperation local approval, Tasking feedback) if interface contracts are not explicitly enforced at implementation time.
Audit history may grow unbounded if retention and archival policy is not implemented with automated cleanup, compression, or cold storage mechanisms.
Telemetry may perform context enrichment or filtering under the guise of 'normalization' if the raw context contract is not precisely defined and validated at integration.

Performance target caching in Vehicle or Teleoperation subsystems may occur as a performance optimization without explicit prohibition if the single-source-of-truth constraint is not enforced via interface design (e.g., read-only access, versioning, invalidation signals).
evidence_needed: Telemetry interface specification documenting the exact schema and content of raw operational context delivered to Safety Services (vehicle state variables, environmental sensor readings, mission parameters, timestamps).
Safety Services latency measurement baseline and SLA definition with acceptance criteria for automated parsing and expert validation gating.
SafetyAlert interface specification documenting approval signal routing, audit trail capture, and bidirectional communication protocol.
Tasking interface specification documenting performance target consumption, versioning, and prohibition of local caching or fallback derivation.
Audit history schema and retention policy specification within Safety Services (e.g., rolling window size, compression algorithm, archival schedule, compliance record-keeping duration).
Worksite safety plan and operational policies documentation mapped to automated parsing rules and expert validation criteria in Safety Services design specification.

Integration test plan validating that Telemetry delivers raw context without pre-processing, SafetyAlert routes all approvals through Safety Services, and no subsystem implements local target derivation or caching.
Audit trail inspection results demonstrating that all performance target derivations, approvals, and revisions are logged with timestamps, actor identities, and justifications.
